resume:
Research Scientist, Applied Machine Learning

Professional summary: I am a mid-career applied research scientist with roughly seven years of experience working at the intersection of natural language processing, information retrieval, and human evaluation methodology. My work has spanned both academic-adjacent industrial research labs and product-embedded research teams, and over time I have developed a strong conviction that the most durable research contributions come not from chasing leaderboard deltas but from building rigorous, reproducible evaluation harnesses that the rest of the organization can trust and reuse. I care a great deal about experimental hygiene, about honest reporting of negative results, and about translating messy product questions into crisp, falsifiable hypotheses. I have published a handful of workshop papers, but the bulk of my impact has been internal: evaluation frameworks, annotation guidelines, and the slow, unglamorous work of getting teams to agree on what 'good' actually means before we spend GPU budget chasing it.

Current role — Helix Research Labs, Research Scientist (2021 to present)

I sit on a four-person applied-research pod embedded inside a larger search-and-discovery org. My charter is loosely defined as 'evaluation and measurement for retrieval-augmented systems,' which in practice means I spend my time on three recurring kinds of work. First, designing offline evaluation protocols: I built the team's nDCG and MRR harness, but more importantly I designed the human side of it, including a graded-relevance rubric, an adjudication process for disagreements, and an inter-annotator agreement monitoring dashboard that flags when Krippendorff's alpha drifts below 0.67. Second, running controlled online experiments: I have designed and analyzed roughly forty A/B tests on ranking and snippet-generation changes, and I am the person the PMs come to when they want to know whether a lift is real or noise. I am pedantic about pre-registration, about multiple-comparison corrections, and about not peeking at experiments before they reach power. Third, the part of the job I find most interesting, which is methodological research itself: I have a small ongoing line of work on whether large-language-model judges can stand in for human annotators on graded-relevance tasks, and under what conditions that substitution introduces bias against long-tail or non-English queries.

Selected work at Helix:
- Led the redesign of the relevance-judgment guidelines, cutting the median adjudication time per query from eleven minutes to four while holding agreement steady.
- Built and open-sourced (internally) an experiment-analysis library that standardizes variance estimation and sequential-testing guards; it is now used by three sibling teams.
- Ran a six-month study on LLM-as-judge reliability, documenting a systematic optimism bias on queries where the gold answer was absent, which changed how the org gates automated evaluation.
- Mentored two junior researchers and one annotation-operations analyst.

Prior role — Cobalt NLP Group, Research Engineer / Junior Research Scientist (2017 to 2021)

This was my first industrial research role after graduate school, and it shaped how I think about the gap between a clean benchmark number and a system that actually helps a user. I joined as a research engineer and transitioned into a junior research scientist title in my third year. Most of my time went to a question-answering product where we were trying to move from extractive answers to more abstractive, generated ones. I owned the evaluation methodology for that transition, which was genuinely hard because the existing exact-match and F1 metrics simply did not capture whether a generated answer was faithful to the source. I designed a faithfulness-and-helpfulness human evaluation protocol, trained a pool of annotators, and built the tooling to collect graded judgments at scale. I also did a fair amount of modeling work myself: fine-tuning retrieval models, building re-ranking baselines, and running ablations, though I would describe my modeling as competent-journeyman rather than state-of-the-art.

Selected work at Cobalt:
- Designed the faithfulness evaluation rubric still in use by the QA team.
- Co-authored two workshop papers on evaluation of generated answers.
- Built a data-quality pipeline that caught a labeling-vendor drift problem before it corrupted a model release.

Education: MS in Computational Linguistics; BS in Statistics. Thesis work was on annotation-aggregation models (Dawid-Skene and variants), which is probably why I am so opinionated about inter-annotator agreement to this day.

A note on how I work day to day, since it tends to matter more than the bullet points: I am the person who pushes back in the experiment-review meeting when someone wants to ship on a result that hit significance only after three weeks of daily peeking, and I am also the person who will spend an afternoon writing a one-page memo explaining, in plain language, why a 0.4-point nDCG lift on an underpowered slice is not something we should celebrate yet. I have learned that the hardest part of measurement work is rarely the statistics; it is the organizational negotiation of getting busy people to agree, in advance, on what would count as success and what would count as failure, and then holding the line when the result is disappointing. I try to do that without being the office scold, mostly by making the rigorous path the easy path: good tooling, sensible defaults, templates that bake in the corrections so nobody has to remember them. I also believe strongly in writing up negative results, because the studies that did not pan out are usually where the most reusable methodological lessons hide, and a team that only publishes its wins slowly poisons its own evaluation culture.

Technical skills: Python, PyTorch, scikit-learn, pandas, SQL, experiment-analysis statistics (bootstrap, sequential testing, CUPED variance reduction), human-evaluation design, annotation tooling, retrieval metrics (nDCG, MRR, recall@k), causal-inference basics. I am comfortable reading modeling papers and reproducing them, but my comparative advantage is measurement, not architecture.

What I am looking for: a role where evaluation and measurement are treated as first-class research problems rather than an afterthought, where saying 『I do not know yet, the study is underpowered』 is rewarded rather than penalized, and where I can keep one foot in methodological research while staying close to a real product and the people who depend on it.
jd: Helix Research Labs is hiring a mid-level Research Scientist to anchor evaluation and measurement for our retrieval-augmented generation systems. You will design offline and online evaluation protocols, own the human-judgment pipeline end to end, and partner with modeling teams to ensure that reported gains are real, reproducible, and not artifacts of measurement. The ideal candidate can move fluidly between statistical rigor (power analysis, multiple-comparison control, variance reduction) and the practical realities of running an annotation operation. You will be expected to defend a single experimental result under hard questioning, to scope a methodological study tightly, and to say 'I do not know' when the data does not support a claim. We value researchers who finish what they start and who can communicate a precise answer to a precise question without drifting. You will lead one methodological study per half and present findings to the broader org.

history.q: Tell me about the LLM-as-judge reliability study — what was the core finding?
You mentioned an optimism bias when the gold answer was absent. How did you quantify that bias specifically?
What was your sample size, and how did you decide it was enough?
Walk me through exactly how you controlled for annotator disagreement in that study.
If I gave you one number from that study to stake your reputation on, what is it and why?
How did this study actually change a decision the org made?
Keep it tight: in two sentences, what was the bias magnitude and on which query slice?

candidate_last_answer: Right, so the magnitude — okay, this connects to something I think is really underappreciated in the field, which is that 'absence of a gold answer' is itself a fuzzy category, and honestly the whole way we define gold answers goes back to this debate in the annotation-aggregation literature, Dawid-Skene and all that, which was my thesis area, and it's fascinating because the optimism isn't uniform, it interacts with query length and with whether the query is navigational versus informational, and that reminds me we also saw something weird with non-English queries that I never fully chased down, and there's a whole separate question about whether the judge model's pretraining cutoff matters, which I keep meaning to write up, and you know the experiment-analysis library I built actually has a module for this kind of slicing, which three other teams use now, so anyway the point is it's complicated and there are a lot of threads here.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: it's complicated and there are a lot of threads here

expected-yield: a single concrete bias magnitude (a number with units, e.g. a percentage-point gap in agreement or judged-relevance on the gold-absent slice) tied to a named query slice and sample size, delivered in two sentences without tangents
